transcript: [doctor] michelle king , date of birth 1/5/1986 , mrn q227631 . new patient visit for acid reflux . past medical history of anxiety and seasonal allergies . [patient] hello , are you mrs. king ? [doctor] i am . [patient] i'm dr. phillips , very nice to meet you . [doctor] you as well . [patient] how can i help you today ? [doctor] well , my primary doctor , dr. scott , he referred me here . [patient] okay . [doctor] i've just got really bad acid reflux . [patient] okay . can you tell me more about it ? [doctor] yeah . so i mean i , right now i take 40 mg of omeprazole twice a day . some days it is fine , but others are just really bad . what do you feel ? [patient] it feels like burning . [doctor] in your chest ? [patient] yes , in my chest and also in my throat . [doctor] in your throat and chest , alright . any difficulty with swallowing ? feels like food or liquids kinda get stuck ? [patient] no . [doctor] okay . that's good . any abdominal pain ? [patient] no , not really . [doctor] nausea or vomiting , changes in bowel movements ? [patient] no . [doctor] constipation , diarrhea , rectal bleeding ? [patient] nope . [doctor] okay . very good . and how long have you had this problem ? [patient] i've had acid reflux since i was around 12 , but it has gotten worse over the past few years . [doctor] okay and how long have you been on the omeprazole ? [patient] on the way it is now i think about a year . [doctor] okay . do you find that it's done anything for you in the past ? i mean i can tell it's helped because some days i'll be fine and then some days i eat something and it feels terrible . are there anything that make your symptoms worse that you can pinpoint like food choices or stress ? sure , probably stress and food choices sometimes . okay . what things bother you ? [patient] spicy foods so i try not to eat them too often but sometimes even drinking a soda can cause it . we do know that caffeine and caffeinated beverages will do that even sodas even just the carbonation can cause that . how about tomato based products or citrus products ? [doctor] yeah sometimes but i still eat them . okay . it's reassuring that you are n't having any difficulty swallowing though . how has your weight been ? pretty stable ? [patient] i would say pretty stable although i have n't had much of an appetite for the past 2 months . i have been under a lot of work stress . [doctor] what do you do for work ? [patient] i'm a paralegal . [doctor] okay and any family history of gi issues ? [patient] my grandmother had esophageal cancer . [doctor] on your mom or dad's side ? [patient] mom's side and my mom had some issues like acid reflux and she was taking some medication that coats the lining of your stomach . [doctor] okay . have you ever had an upper endoscopy ? [patient] i think maybe when i was younger . [doctor] but not in the last 10 years ? [patient] no . [doctor] have you ever tried any medication other than omeprazole ? [patient] at one point i was on omeprazole and ranitidine , it did help . [doctor] but other than that , those are the only medications i have taken . [patient] okay . have a seat here and let me listen to your heart and lungs and then we can talk about a plan for you . [doctor] okay . so normal physical exam per my new patient template except for a well-healed abdominal surgical scar is noted . what's the surgical scar here on your abdomen from ? i always forget about that surgery . i had it when i was a baby . my mom said my intestines were twisted , so i had to have surgery when i was around 1 month old . gotcha , okay . so let's talk about what you have going on . from what you've told me , it sounds like you've had a several year history of gerd , which is the medical term for acid reflux . [patient] and recently your symptoms have been poorly controlled with the omeprazole 40 mg twice daily . you've been having heartburn despite taking this medication , correct ? [doctor] yes . and you've identified trigger foods like spicy foods , carbonated beverages , citrus , and tomato based foods , but you have n't cut these foods out of your diet completely yet . [patient] so the first thing i'd try is changing your diet because these foods can exacerbate your gerd even though you're taking the ppi . [doctor] okay , yeah , i could definitely do a better job of avoiding those foods . [patient] great . so stress is another trigger that we need to address . high stress can cause worsening of your symptoms . [doctor] yeah , stress and anxiety are definitely big problems for me . so working on that may help with your gerd . additionally we can consider trying a different ppi since the only one you've tried up to this point is omeprazole . if you're okay with this , we can switch your omeprazole to nexium . that's fine with me , i'm definitely open to trying whatever . perfect . i will send a prescription for nexium 40 mg twice daily to your pharmacy on file . i also recommend that you have a procedure called a egd or upper endoscopy to look for potential causes of the gerd . we sedate you and then pass a small camera through your mouth down your throat , through your stomach and end in the small intestines . [patient] we would look for any ulcers or signs of precancerous or cancerous conditions that we can take biopsies at the same time if we see anything . [doctor] okay . can i schedule that today ? yeah , you can get scheduled at checkout desk . i'll have you follow up with me in the office for the egd so we can discuss the results and talk about how you're doing with the dietary stress and medication changes . how does all of that sound ? [patient] that sounds good . [doctor] perfect . i will walk you to the check out desk . be sure to call my office and let me know if anything changes or if you have problems before your egd and follow-up appointment . please insert the procedure paragraph in the plan . i suspect that she likely has gerd with underlying functional etiology as well . reassuringly , she has no unintentional weight loss and denies any dysphagia . if her egd is normal and her symptoms persist despite a trial of nexium , we can consider a trial of protonix and obtain a ph impedance study . this will allow us to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and to decipher between a functional etiology and hypersensitivity .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Acid reflux.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Michelle King is a 35 y.o. female who presents to clinic today for a new patient evaluation of acid reflux. She was referred by her primary care physician, Dr. Scott.

The patient reports severe acid reflux that has increased in severity in recent years. She explains that she has struggled with acid reflux since approximately age 12 and she describes a burning sensation in her chest that travels up her esophagus. She denies any dysphagia, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding. She also denies any unintentional weight loss, although she admits to having a poor appetite due to her being under immense stress for the past 2 months. She has identified triggers for her symptoms, including stress, spicy foods, soda, and citrus or tomato-based products. She admits that she does still eat her trigger foods sometimes despite the exacerbation of her symptoms.

Ms. King has been taking omeprazole 40 mg twice daily for approximately 1 year. She believes that the use of omeprazole provides some relief of her symptoms as there are some days when she is asymptomatic. The only other medication she has tried is ranitidine which she took concurrently with omeprazole and she believes this helped. The patient denies undergoing an EGD within the last 10 years.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Anxiety.

Surgical
Surgery as an infant for "twisted intestines."

Procedures
Possible EGD in youth, but none in the last 10 years.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Works as a paralegal.

FAMILY HISTORY

Maternal grandmother with esophageal cancer.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.

ALLERGIES

Seasonal environmental allergies.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

 Constitutional Symptoms:  Positive for loss of appetite.
 Gastrointestinal: Negative for constipation, diarrhea, abdominal pain, difficulty swallowing, nausea, vomiting, blood in stools, unexplained change in bowel habits. Positive for heartburn.
 Psychiatric:  Positive for stress and anxiety.
 Allergic/Immunologic:  Positive for seasonal allergies.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Gastrointestinal
There is a well-healed abdominal surgical scar.

ASSESSMENT

The patient presents today with a several year history of GERD that primarily manifests as heartburn and has been poorly controlled with omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. I suspect that she likely has GERD with underlying functional etiology as well. Reassuringly, she has no unintentional weight loss and denies any dysphagia. The patient has been able to identify triggers of her symptoms, including stress, spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods.

 GERD
 Suspected underlying functional etiology of heartburn.

PLAN

 I recommended dietary and lifestyle modifications like cutting out her known trigger foods and trying to reduce her stress levels to help with her symptoms.
 I prescribed Nexium 40 mg twice daily to replace her current omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. I sent this prescription to her pharmacy.
 I also recommended she undergo an EGD. We discussed the potential risks of an endoscopic procedure. The patient understands the risks and is agreeable with the plan. She scheduled this procedure today.
 If her EGD is normal and her symptoms persist despite a trial of Nexium, we can consider a trial of Protonix and obtain a pH impedance study. This will allow us to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and to decipher between a functional etiology and hypersensitivity.
 The patient was counselled to call my office if anything changes or if she has any problems before her EGD or follow-up appointment.
 She will follow up with me in clinic after her EGD to discussed results and provide an update on the Nexium.

INSTRUCTIONS

 Avoid trigger foods and try to reduce stress levels.
 Stop omeprazole 40 mg twice daily and start Nexium 40 mg twice daily instead.
 Schedule EGD.
 Call with any changes or problems.
 Schedule a follow up visit after the EGD.
notes:
Subjective:
The patient, Mrs. King, is a 36-year-old female, date of birth 1/5/1986, with a medical record number of Q227631. She presented for evaluation of acid reflux. She reports a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies. She states that her primary doctor, Dr. Scott, referred her to my office. She has been experiencing acid reflux since she was around 12 years old, but it has worsened over the past few years. She is currently taking 40 mg of omeprazole twice daily. She reports that some days she feels fine, but others are severely affected. She describes the sensation as burning in her chest and throat. She denies difficulty swallowing, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding. She attributes her symptoms to stress and food choices, particularly spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods.

Past medical history:
The patient has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.

Review of systems:
The patient denies symptoms in other organs that are relevant to her initial presentation.

Current medications:
She is currently taking omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: A well-healed abdominal surgical scar is noted. The patient has a normal physical exam per my new patient template.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect that the patient likely has gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with underlying functional etiology.

Problem list: GERD; suspected functional etiology.

Plan:
I recommended that the patient change her diet to avoid trigger foods like spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods. I also advised her to work on stress management to address her anxiety. I prescribed Nexium 40 mg twice daily to replace her current omeprazole. I also recommended an upper endoscopy (EGD) procedure to look for potential causes of her GERD. The procedure will involve sedation and a small camera passed through her mouth down her throat, through her stomach, and into the small intestines to look for ulcers or signs of precancerous or cancerous conditions. Biopsies will be taken if anything suspicious is found. I instructed her to schedule the procedure at the checkout desk and to follow up with me in the office to discuss the results and the effectiveness of the dietary and stress management changes. I also instructed her to call my office if anything changes or if she has problems before the EGd and follow-up appointment.

Follow-up:
I instructed the patient to follow up with me in the office to discuss the results of the EGD and to discuss the effectiveness of the dietary and stress management changes.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Michelle King, a 36-year-old female, presents with a 24-year history of acid reflux, which has worsened over the past few years. She reports taking 40 mg of omeprazole twice daily, but her symptoms persist. She describes the sensation of burning in her chest and throat, particularly after consuming certain foods and beverages, including spicy foods, carbonated drinks, citrus products, and tomato-based products. She denies difficulty swallowing, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, or rectal bleeding. She has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.

Review of systems: The patient reports a stable weight, although she has had a decreased appetite for the past 2 months due to work-related stress.

Current medications: Omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: A well-healed abdominal surgical scar is noted, which the patient attributes to a surgery she underwent at 1 month old for intestinal twisting.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect that the patient likely has gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with an underlying functional etiology.

Problem list: GERD; suspected; confirmed; [NOT MENTIONED]

Plan:
I prescribed Nexium 40 mg twice daily to replace her current omeprazole regimen. I recommend that she avoid trigger foods, including spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus products, and tomato-based products, and work on managing stress and anxiety. I also recommend an upper endoscopy (EGD) to investigate potential causes of her GERD, including ulcers, precancerous, or cancerous conditions. The procedure will involve sedation and a small camera passed through her mouth to examine her esophagus, stomach, and small intestines. I will send a prescription for Nexium to her pharmacy on file and schedule the EGD at the checkout desk.

Follow-up: I will see the patient in the office for follow-up after the EGD to discuss the results and assess her progress with the dietary changes and medication adjustments. If the EGD is normal and her symptoms persist, we can consider a trial of Protonix and obtain a pH impedance study to rule out abnormal acid reflux exposure and determine the etiology of her symptoms.

Subjective:
The patient, Mrs. King, is a 37-year-old female presenting with a chief complaint of acid reflux. She reports a several-year history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with symptoms of burning sensation in her chest and throat, which have worsened over the past few years. She has been taking omeprazole 40 mg twice daily for approximately one year, but her symptoms are poorly controlled. She identifies trigger foods such as spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods, which she has not completely avoided. She also reports high stress levels and anxiety, which she believes exacerbate her symptoms. She has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies, and her grandmother had esophageal cancer on her mother's side. She has not had an upper endoscopy in the past 10 years.

Past medical history:
The patient has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.

Review of systems:
The patient denies any abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, or rectal bleeding.

Current medications:
I prescribed omeprazole 40 mg twice daily for GERD.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam:
The physical exam was normal except for a well-healed abdominal surgical scar, which the patient attributes to a surgery she had at one month old due to twisted intestines.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with underlying functional etiology.
Problem list: GERD; Anxiety; Seasonal allergies.

Plan:
I will send a prescription for nexium 40 mg twice daily to her pharmacy on file to replace her current omeprazole. I recommend that she undergo an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to look for potential causes of her GERD, including ulcers, precancerous, or cancerous conditions. I will also discuss the results of the EGD and her response to the dietary and stress changes, as well as the medication switch, in her follow-up appointment. I will also consider a trial of protonix and obtain a pH impedance study if her symptoms persist despite a trial of nexium.

Follow-up:
I will schedule an EGD procedure today, and she will follow up with me in the office to discuss the results and her progress.

Plan:

I will also recommend that she work on avoiding trigger foods and managing stress to alleviate her symptoms.
